label: What is a Leave & Earnings Statement (LES) and how do I get one?
content:
An LES is a document provided to all U.S. service members at the end of every month which summarizes their pay for that period. It details entitlements like base pay and Basic Allowance for Housing (BAH), as well as deductions such as federal and state taxes or SGLI, and any direct payments to other entities. An LES also includes variables like the number of dependents and tax filing status, which PayLES uses to tailor financial analysis to each user's unique situation.

It can be accessed at the MyPay.mil website or through your unit's finance office.

label: LES documents have personal information, how does PayLES handle this?
content: PayLES does not read any Personally Identifiable Information (PII) data, name and SSN, from the LES. All data read is captured only from specific bounding boxes on the LES which are described in the les_rect_text.csv file. Additionally, the pdf is converted into a png image in the les.py file for display on the page with box modal overlays. During this conversion, the name and SSN have whiteout applied over them, and the png is flattened to ensure that the data cannot be extracted.

label: Is PayLES secure?
content:
PayLES is as secure as any other web application. It does not store any user data, and all data is processed in-memory without saving to disk. The application does not require any user accounts or personal information, ensuring privacy and security. Upon switching to another tab or closing the browser, all data is wiped from memory. PayLES also uses HTTPS to encrypt data in transit, providing an extra layer of security.

With PayLES being open-source, every single line of code is available for anyone to review, ensuring transparency and trustworthiness. The source code is hosted on GitHub. Additionally, the developer is commited to maintaining the security and privacy of the application as technology updates and will promptly address any security vulnerabilities or concerns that may arise.

label: Is PayLES truly free?
content:
Yes, PayLES is completely free to use! There are no paywalled features, subscriptions, or user information collected or stored (see the security FAQ above). This is because the developer pays for all of the costs required to run PayLES themselves.

To start with a breakdown of the finances, the domain payles.app costs $14.20/year from CloudFlare. For development, a GitHub CoPilot subscription is used which costs $10.73/month. PayLES uses Render, a web hosting service, for $7/month at the starter tier. This guarantees 24/7 uptime and provides 0.5 CPU and 512 MB RAM ensuring decently fast performance for users.

The developer uses PayLES himself to budget paying out-of-pocket for all of these expenses and intends to do so for as long as financially viable. The current amount of users is within the limits of the starter tier, however if the user base grows significantly then the developer will purchase increasing tiers of performance as needed. If the costs ever become too high, the developer may consider implementing a donation system to help cover costs, but there are currently no plans to do so. The developer would rather users make donations to military charities (see the contribution FAQ below) than to PayLES.

label: What are the limitations of PayLES?
content:
PayLES has several limitations users should be aware of. First, PayLES does not store any user data, meaning that all data is lost upon refreshing or closing the browser. Users must re-upload their LES and re-enter any adjustments each time they visit the site. To help mitigate this, the export feature allows users to download their entire budget as an excel or CSV file for offline use.

Secondly, PayLES does not currently take age into account when performing TSP calculations. Age matters when contributing to a TSP account, such as increasing contribution limits for those over 50 and 60. There are no plans to make this a feature as only approximately 6% of the military is over 50, and doesn't seem necessary as the increased limit can easily be hand-calculated.

Third, PayLES does not conduct pro-rated calculations, it treats all months as the same. This means that changes which occur in the middle of the month may not be accurately reflected in the calculations. For example, if a user promotes in the middle of the month, their pay will either display the current month as having the full promotion, or the next month. Difficulty of implementation is the largest factor preventing this feature.

Fourth, PayLES is not designed for transactions tracking to get actual realized data, like adding to your monthly gas total each time you fill up your vehicle. It does not tie into any banking institution to see your as-is accounts and transactions, sacrificing convenience and accuracy for simplicity and security. There are no plans to integrate bank accounts and transaction tracking.

And finally, PayLES does not account for all possible pay scenarios and may not be able to accurately estimate pay for users with very unique situations.

label: How accurate is PayLES?
content:
PayLES strives to be as accurate as possible, however is unable to guarantee 100% accuracy of calculations. PayLES uses official U.S. government and DoW datasets and algorithms to provide estimates based on the information provided by the user, however each user's situation is unique and all factors cannot be accounted for. The largest causes of deviations are surrounding edge cases such as unique OCONUS assignments, unpaid deductions, and special National Guard/Reserve drill calculations. Additionally, PayLES has limitations in determining whether certain pay is an error or not, sometimes resulting in payment mistakes going unrecognized or treated as being accurate. PayLES strives to mitigate this by analyzing all calculations against the uploaded LES and flagging any discrepancies for user review.

Users should always verify their PayLES budget against their actual LES each month to ensure accuracy. If any discrepancies are found, please report them through the GitHub issues page or contact me through email so that they can be investigated and corrected.

label: How can I get the data sources used in PayLES?
content: All data sources used in PayLES are listed in the data_sources.csv file in the GitHub repository. This file lists the data as well as the links used to source the data which all are derived from official U.S. government and DoW sites to ensure accuracy and reliability. If you have any questions regarding the authenticity of data sources or would like to contribute additional data sources, please feel free to submit an issue or pull request on GitHub.

label: What was PayLES developed with?
content: PayLES is built with Python and the Flask web framework for backend data processing, while HTML, CSS, and JavaScript power the frontend interface. This technology stack ensures the application is fast, lightweight, and responsive, accessible from any modern web browser. Render, a web hosting company, is used to deploy the application with a custom domain name and SSL certificate provided by Cloudflare. To see all code used to build PayLES, please visit the GitHub repository at GitHub.

label: Was any AI used to make PayLES?
content: PayLES is made with the help of Github Copilot using GPT-4.1 LLM. Copilot is used because GitHub is providing hosting capabilities and version control for the project, it's easily integrated into the Visual Studio Code IDE, and overall is an effective tool helping speed up development. Copilot was only used for code generation, documentation, and providing suggestions for improving the quality of the codebase. However, approximately 50% of the code was written without Copilot, and of the code that was generated about half would have been written regardless of Copilot's assistance. All ideas, core logic, and design decisions were made by the developer.

label: What is the future roadmap for PayLES?
content:
The current focus is on improving the user experience by ensuring high accuracy, expanding the amount of unique use cases captured, and maintaining up to date datasets. Some features currently being worked are better accounting of OCONUS payments, joint LES upload, and ability to create reports and graphs. For the long-term, the developer would like to explore the possibility of having PayLES become an official DoW application. The goal of this would be to increase accessibility, availability, and accuracy of the program, especially if it was able to be supported by DFAS or another organization.

There are currently no plans to incorporate mobile support, whether through mobile browsing or an app. Having user accounts that save the budget allowing for convenient future access is also not being considered at this time. The scope is also going to remain focused on military pay, and not expand so far into personal budgeting such as tracking individual expenses over a monthly period.

label: Who developed PayLES?
content:
PayLES is developed by me, Mike West. I am an active duty engineering officer in the United States Air Force, and work on this project entirely off-duty in my spare time. I have spent well over 500 hours on this project, ranging from research, design, programming, testing, and deploying.

I started this project while attending an applied project course at Arizona State University in early 2025, and it started out as a simple LES reader with basic calculations. However, as I continued to learn more about military finances I realized that there was a significant capability gap for military members needing to understand the LES and military pay structure. The project has since evolved into a comprehensive, personalized budgeting application that tailors to unique situations and provides detailed analysis and recommendations to users.

I originally did not have my name associated with this project since I wished to remain anonymous for privacy reasons and the fact that I do not intend to achieve any fame, recognition, or money from PayLES. This project is strictly for the benefit of other service members. However, I have since decided to include my name as I believe it's important to be transparent about who is behind this project and to take full responsibility for its development and maintenance. If you have any questions or concerns please feel free to contact me at payles.contact@pm.me or through GitHub. I welcome all feedback!

label: How can I contribute to this project?
content:
The best way to contribute to this project is by just simply spreading the word! If you find PayLES useful, please share it with others. The more people who use PayLES means the more feedback received and improvements made.

I also welcome all technical contributions, whether reporting any bugs or issues, suggesting new features, or providing feedback on the user experience. If you would like to contribute code, please feel free to submit a pull request on GitHub.

However, if you are non-technical but are truly determined to make an impactful contribution, please consider making a donation to a military charity on behalf of PayLES. Some of my favorite charities include the USO and Fisher House Foundation, but there are many great organizations that are always in need of support.

Thank you.
